skill.name: skywork-design
skill.description: Generate and edit visual assets with the Skywork Image API, including posters, logos, product images, brochures, storyboards, infographics, and social media graphics.
skill.summary: Create and edit visual design assets through Skywork image generation workflows.
skill.author: SkyworkAI

security_audit.summary: Static findings in markdown scenario files are mostly false positives from fenced commands, design terms, and markdown text. The Python scripts confirm high-risk behavior: they call external Skywork services, read and write local files, cache authentication tokens in the home directory, and launch browser commands. No prompt injection attempt was found, but publication should require manual trust review and clear user consent.

high_findings.title: Plaintext Authentication Token Cached in Home Directory
high_findings.description: The authentication module stores Skywork token data in ~/.skywork_token and later reloads it for API use. This is functional but risky because no file permission hardening or encryption is shown for a reusable credential.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The token path, save operation, and reuse flow are directly visible. The risk is credential exposure rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

high_findings.title: Credential-Bearing Requests to Configurable External Services
high_findings.description: The image generator sends a token header to the configured gateway endpoint, and API base URLs can be changed through environment variables. In an untrusted runtime, endpoint override could expose tokens, prompts, and uploaded image data.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network destinations are production defaults but are environment-overridable. The code clearly attaches credentials to outbound requests.

medium_findings.title: Local Images Are Uploaded to Remote API
medium_findings.description: When editing images, the script reads user-supplied local files, base64-encodes them, and includes them in a remote API request. This is expected for image editing but creates a privacy risk for sensitive local images.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file read and request payload construction are explicit. The behavior is legitimate for this skill, so the issue is consent and data handling.

medium_findings.title: Automatic Browser Launch Uses Subprocess
medium_findings.description: The login flow opens the Skywork login URL with platform-specific subprocess calls. The URL is printed for manual login, but subprocess execution is still a sensitive behavior in a community skill.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess calls are visible and appear intended only to open a browser. Injection risk is limited, but Windows uses shell=True and the base URL is configurable.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command and Backtick Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Many external command alerts point to fenced command examples in SKILL.md and inline markdown in scenario files. These are instructions for using the skill, not Ruby or shell execution embedded in application code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are markdown documentation and tables. I found no Ruby code in those files.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: Weak cryptographic algorithm alerts in scenario markdown and SKILL.md match ordinary design words, file names, examples, or markdown content. No evidence found of weak cryptographic implementation in those markdown files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations are prose about visual design and content integrity. There is no cryptographic API use at those lines.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Alerts Are Mostly Benign Context Checks
low_findings.description: The platform check selects the correct browser opener for login, and scenario uses of the word system refer to design systems. This is not host reconnaissance beyond basic OS detection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only real system call is platform.system used for browser selection. The markdown references are design language.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network API Calls With Token Header
dangerous_patterns.description: The generator obtains a Skywork token and sends it in a token header on outbound POST requests.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The token retrieval and request header are directly shown in the same function.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local File Upload Encoding
dangerous_patterns.description: Input images are read from disk, encoded as base64, and added to the image editing request body.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file read and base64 payload creation are explicit. This is expected behavior but still sensitive.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hidden Token Cache File
dangerous_patterns.description: The auth module uses a hidden file in the user home directory to persist token data.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The hidden path and read/write functions are directly visible.

dangerous_patterns.title: Subprocess Browser Launcher
dangerous_patterns.description: The auth flow invokes OS-specific commands to open the login URL in a browser.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess calls are explicit. Their intent appears limited to browser launch, so this is a pattern risk rather than confirmed abuse.

content.value_statement: Teams need fast visual assets but often lack a repeatable design workflow. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Skywork image generation and editing for common marketing and brand tasks.
actual_capabilities: Generates new images from text prompts through the Skywork Image API.
Edits one or more local reference images with prompt-based instructions.
Supports 1K, 2K, and 4K resolution options for generated assets.
Supports common aspect ratios for social, print, video, and banner formats.
Provides scenario guidance for logos, branding, posters, brochures, infographics, e-commerce images, social media, and storyboards.
Downloads generated image files locally and prints a remote OSS URL.
limitations: Requires Skywork authentication before image generation can run.
Uploads prompts and input images to external Skywork services.
Generated text inside images may need review for accuracy and legibility.
Large or high-resolution generations can take minutes and may depend on account benefits.

use_cases.title: Create campaign visuals
use_cases.description: Generate posters, social posts, and banner images with consistent tone, aspect ratio, and resolution.

use_cases.title: Develop brand concepts
use_cases.description: Explore logo marks, color palettes, typography showcases, and brand mockups from a short creative brief.

use_cases.title: Prepare product listing images
use_cases.description: Create product hero shots, lifestyle scenes, feature callouts, and package content images for online stores.

prompt_templates.title: Generate a simple image
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a 1:1 social media image for [topic]. Use a clear focal point, [style] visual style, and [main colors]. Save it as [filename].

prompt_templates.title: Edit a reference image
prompt_templates.prompt: Edit the attached image to [specific change]. Preserve the main subject, lighting direction, and composition. Use 2K resolution and save the result as [filename].

prompt_templates.title: Build a brand asset set
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a brand concept for [brand name] in [industry]. Start with a logo, then create a color palette card and stationery mockup after approval.

prompt_templates.title: Produce a multi-image campaign
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a campaign image set for [audience] across [platforms]. Define a shared visual system, generate the first asset, then use it as reference for the remaining formats.

output_examples.input: Create an event poster for a product launch with a premium technology look.
output_examples.output: A 3:4 poster image is generated with a strong hero visual, large headline area, launch details, and a clean call-to-action section.

output_examples.input: Edit this product photo into a marketplace-ready main listing image.
output_examples.output: The product is placed on a pure white background, centered with studio lighting, clear edges, and a high-resolution output file.

output_examples.input: Create three LinkedIn post images for a research report launch.
output_examples.output: A consistent social media set is produced with readable headlines, matching colors, safe-zone composition, and platform-appropriate aspect ratios.

best_practices: Give a clear creative brief with audience, format, style, colors, and required text.
Use reference images when brand consistency or character consistency matters.
Review generated images for text accuracy, privacy, brand fit, and platform requirements before publishing.
anti_patterns: Do not upload confidential images unless the user accepts external API processing.
Do not ask for many unrelated styles in one prompt because results may become inconsistent.
Do not rely on generated images for exact legal, medical, financial, or compliance text.

faq.question: Does this skill require an external service?
faq.answer: Yes. It uses Skywork API endpoints for authentication, image generation, image editing, and result downloads.

faq.question: Can it edit existing images?
faq.answer: Yes. It can send one or more local reference images with edit instructions to the Skywork image editing endpoint.

faq.question: Where are generated images saved?
faq.answer: The script saves the downloaded image to the filename provided by the user and prints a remote OSS URL.

faq.question: What formats does it support?
faq.answer: It supports common aspect ratios such as 1:1, 3:4, 4:5, 9:16, 16:9, and 21:9.

faq.question: Is it safe for private images?
faq.answer: Use caution. Input images and prompts are sent to Skywork services, so sensitive content needs explicit user approval.

faq.question: Why is the security rating high?
faq.answer: The skill handles tokens, local files, external API requests, and subprocess browser launch commands in a community package.
